heroOverview: Eosinophilia is an elevated count of eosinophils — a type of white blood cell — above 500 cells per microlitre of blood, affecting an estimated 1-2% of the general population in high-income countries and a far higher proportion in tropical regions where parasitic infections are endemic. Eosinophils form part of the immune system's defence against parasites and modulate allergic inflammation; when their numbers rise persistently, they can deposit toxic granule proteins in tissues, causing damage to the heart, lungs, skin, and nervous system. Most mild cases are driven by a recognisable trigger such as asthma, allergic disease, or medication and carry an excellent prognosis once the cause is treated. Severe or organ-infiltrating eosinophilia — called hypereosinophilic syndrome when the count exceeds 1,500 per microlitre with tissue involvement — requires specialist management.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 1-2% of adults in high-income countries; up to 10-20% in populations from tropical regions with high parasite burden
keyStats.demographics: Can occur at any age; reactive eosinophilia predominates in children in endemic countries; hypereosinophilic syndrome is more common in men aged 20-50
keyStats.avgAge: No single peak age; clonal variants more often diagnosed in adults over 40
keyStats.globalCases: Parasitic causes account for the majority of cases worldwide, with Ascaris lumbricoides infections alone affecting an estimated 800 million people globally

definition: Eosinophilia (ICD-10: D72.1) is defined as an absolute eosinophil count (AEC) above 500 cells per microlitre on a complete blood count. It is graded as mild (500-1,500/µL), moderate (1,500-5,000/µL), or severe (>5,000/µL). When the AEC exceeds 1,500/µL on two occasions at least one month apart, and there is evidence of eosinophil-mediated end-organ damage without an identifiable secondary cause, the diagnosis is hypereosinophilic syndrome (HES). Eosinophils are granulocytes produced in the bone marrow under the influence of interleukin-5 (IL-5), IL-3, and GM-CSF; they circulate briefly before migrating to tissues. Their granules contain major basic protein, eosinophil cationic protein, eosinophil peroxidase, and neurotoxin, which defend against helminths but damage host tissues when released in excess. Eosinophilia falls under the specialty of hematology, with allergology and infectious disease frequently involved. Secondary (reactive) eosinophilia, driven by an external stimulus, accounts for the vast majority of cases; primary (clonal) eosinophilia, resulting from a haematopoietic stem cell mutation, is rare but more serious.

typesClassification.description: Absolute eosinophil count 500-1,500 per µL; usually reactive, often found incidentally, and carries a low risk of organ damage.
Count 1,500-5,000 per µL; warrants investigation for an identifiable cause such as a helminth infection, medication, or allergic disease; tissue damage possible.
Count above 5,000 per µL; organ involvement (cardiac, pulmonary, neurological) becomes more likely; urgent evaluation needed.
Persistent count above 1,500/µL with confirmed end-organ damage, after excluding known secondary causes; requires disease-modifying therapy.
A neoplastic expansion of eosinophil precursors driven by a somatic mutation, most often the FIP1L1-PDGFRA fusion gene; classified as a myeloid neoplasm and treated with imatinib.
Eosinophilia caused by an identifiable driver — parasite, allergen, drug, or inflammatory disease — that resolves when the underlying cause is treated.

primarySymptoms: Persistent fatigue and malaise that does not resolve with rest, reflecting the systemic inflammatory burden of elevated eosinophil activity.
Recurrent or chronic itching (pruritus) of the skin, sometimes with eczematous rashes, urticaria, or angioedema driven by eosinophil-derived mediators.
Cough, wheeze, or breathlessness, particularly at night or on exertion, caused by eosinophilic infiltration of the airways or lung parenchyma.
Abdominal pain, nausea, bloating, or intermittent diarrhoea from eosinophilic involvement of the gastrointestinal tract or from an underlying parasitic infection.
Chest pain or palpitations signalling early eosinophilic myocarditis, which may be the only cardiac symptom before significant damage has occurred.
Skin thickening, erythema, or papular rash in some forms of hypereosinophilic syndrome and Churg-Strauss (eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis).
Peripheral neuropathy manifesting as numbness, tingling, or weakness in the hands and feet from eosinophilic infiltration of peripheral nerves.
Nasal congestion, rhinorrhoea, and loss of smell in patients whose eosinophilia is driven by allergic rhinitis, nasal polyps, or EGPA.
Oedema of the limbs or face reflecting cardiac dysfunction, venous thrombosis, or the Löffler-type phase of tropical pulmonary eosinophilia.
Fever and night sweats in clonal or secondary eosinophilia associated with lymphoma, drug reaction, or helminth migration.
earlyWarningSigns: An incidental rise in eosinophils on routine blood testing when the person has chronic allergy or takes a new medication.
Mild persistent cough or wheeze in someone without a prior respiratory diagnosis.
Unexplained skin rash lasting more than a few weeks, especially if itching is prominent.
Mild fatigue with a count of 1,000-1,500/µL that is not explained by the person's known conditions.
emergencySigns: Chest pain, shortness of breath, or palpitations with a count above 5,000/µL — possible eosinophilic endomyocarditis, which can cause sudden heart failure.
Acute confusion, focal neurological deficit, or seizure associated with eosinophilia — suggests central nervous system involvement.
Rapidly progressive breathlessness and hypoxia with bilateral infiltrates — acute eosinophilic pneumonia, which can mimic ARDS.
Signs of anaphylaxis or severe angioedema during helminth migration.
New-onset heart failure with eosinophil count above 1,500/µL, which requires emergency echocardiography.

causes.description: Tissue-invasive helminths — including Ascaris, Toxocara, Strongyloides, Trichinella, and filarial species — stimulate a Th2 immune response and IL-5 secretion that drives eosinophil production as the host attempts to kill the parasites; this is the most common global cause of significant eosinophilia.

Asthma, allergic rhinitis, atopic dermatitis, and food allergy are associated with chronic Th2 skewing and IL-5 overproduction, resulting in persistently mild to moderate eosinophilia that parallels disease activity and improves with allergen avoidance or anti-IL-5 therapy.

Many medications — including antibiotics (particularly minocycline and nitrofurantoin), NSAIDs, anticonvulsants, allopurinol, and checkpoint inhibitors — trigger idiosyncratic Th2-mediated responses that produce eosinophilia, sometimes as part of DRESS syndrome (drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms).

Somatic mutations, most notably the FIP1L1-PDGFRA fusion arising from a 4q12 deletion, confer autonomous proliferative drive on eosinophil precursors, producing persistent severe eosinophilia independent of cytokine stimulation and classified as a WHO-defined myeloid neoplasm.

A systemic vasculitis driven by antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies (ANCA) in about 30-40% of cases; EGPA combines adult-onset asthma with eosinophilia and small-vessel inflammation affecting the lungs, sinuses, skin, and kidneys, with IL-5-driven eosinophil activation as a central mechanism.

An aberrant T-cell clone secreting IL-5 constitutively can drive secondary eosinophilia without a haematopoietic mutation; such clones can evolve into overt T-cell lymphoma and require immunophenotyping to detect.

causes.cause: Inflammatory bowel disease and other gastrointestinal disorders
causes.description: Crohn's disease, ulcerative colitis, eosinophilic oesophagitis, and eosinophilic gastroenteritis all involve dysregulated mucosal immunity that recruits eosinophils; eosinophilia may be peripheral as well as tissue-based.

riskFactors.factor: Residence in or travel to helminth-endemic regions
riskFactors.description: Sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, and Latin America carry a high burden of soil-transmitted helminths; individuals from or recently returned from these areas have substantially elevated risk of reactive eosinophilia from larval migration.
A personal or family history of atopic disease reflects chronic Th2 immune skewing and is the leading cause of mild eosinophilia in high-income countries, found in roughly 40-50% of mild cases.
Dozens of commonly prescribed drugs can cause drug-induced eosinophilia; reviewing the medication list is a mandatory step in any diagnostic evaluation.
Idiopathic hypereosinophilic syndrome and clonal FIP1L1-PDGFRA-positive disease are 4-9 times more common in men, suggesting a hormonal or genetic predisposition.
Impaired immunity allows dissemination of helminths such as Strongyloides, which can produce hyperinfection syndrome and massive eosinophilia in people receiving corticosteroids or with advanced HIV.
Systemic lupus erythematosus, rheumatoid arthritis, and eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis are associated with secondary eosinophilia as part of their immune dysregulation.
Lymphomas — particularly Hodgkin lymphoma — and several solid tumours produce cytokines that stimulate eosinophilia; this paraneoplastic pattern can precede a cancer diagnosis.

affectedDemographics: People who have lived in or travelled to tropical or subtropical regions with endemic helminth infections
Children and young adults with atopic diseases in high-income countries
Men aged 20-50, who are disproportionately affected by idiopathic and clonal hypereosinophilic syndrome
Patients on polypharmacy regimens, particularly those taking antibiotics, anticonvulsants, or immunotherapy
People with underlying autoimmune conditions such as lupus or EGPA
Immunocompromised individuals at risk of Strongyloides hyperinfection
diagnosisOverview: Evaluation begins with a full blood count that includes an automated differential and a calculated absolute eosinophil count. Any AEC persistently above 500/µL warrants systematic investigation rather than presumptive reassurance. The history should focus on geographic exposure, recent travel, medications, atopic symptoms, gastrointestinal complaints, and any systemic features such as weight loss or fever that suggest malignancy. Initial blood tests include a comprehensive metabolic panel, inflammatory markers, IgE level, and stool ova-and-parasite examination with Strongyloides serology (the latter because stool microscopy misses up to 70% of Strongyloides infections). Serological testing for other helminths (Toxocara, filaria, Echinococcus) is directed by exposure history. If no secondary cause is identified and the AEC exceeds 1,500/µL, bone marrow biopsy and FISH or PCR for the FIP1L1-PDGFRA fusion gene are performed to exclude clonal disease. Cardiac assessment with echocardiography and troponin is essential at this threshold because silent endomyocarditis can present without symptoms. Flow cytometry to identify an aberrant T-cell clone is part of the workup when lymphocyte-variant HES is suspected. Imaging with CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis helps detect lymphoma, solid tumours, organomegaly, and pulmonary infiltrates. Skin biopsy and bronchoscopy with bronchoalveolar lavage are added when organ-specific involvement is suspected.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies the absolute eosinophil count, grades severity, and reveals concurrent cytopenias or leukocytosis that suggest a clonal process.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results typically returned within hours. An automated differential is confirmed by manual review when eosinophils are elevated.

diagnosticTests.test: Stool ova-and-parasite examination plus Strongyloides serology
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects helminth eggs or larvae in stool; serology adds sensitivity for Strongyloides, which microscopy often misses.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Three separate stool specimens collected on different days are submitted to the laboratory; a blood sample is also drawn for the serology ELISA.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum IgE and specific allergen testing
diagnosticTests.purpose: Elevated total IgE supports an allergic aetiology; specific IgE panels identify the causative allergen.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; total IgE results are available within 24-48 hours. Specific panels may take several days.

diagnosticTests.test: FISH or PCR for FIP1L1-PDGFRA fusion gene
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the most common driver of clonal eosinophilia, which is exquisitely sensitive to imatinib therapy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood or bone marrow sample sent to a molecular laboratory; results in 1-2 weeks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for eosinophilic endomyocarditis — intracardiac thrombi, wall motion abnormalities, or endomyocardial fibrosis — which can occur silently with AEC above 1,500/µL.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Non-invasive ultrasound examination of the heart lasting 30-45 minutes; no radiation involved.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects lymphadenopathy, organomegaly, pulmonary infiltrates, or solid tumours that may explain or be caused by eosinophilia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Scan lasting 10-15 minutes; contrast dye injection is standard to enhance tissue detail. Requires adequate kidney function.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Required when clonal eosinophilia is suspected; evaluates marrow cellularity, morphology, fibrosis, and molecular markers.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A short procedure under local anaesthesia to extract a small sample from the posterior iliac crest; mild soreness persists for 1-2 days.

treatmentOverview: Treatment strategy depends entirely on cause and severity. In reactive eosinophilia, treating the underlying driver — deworming with albendazole or ivermectin for helminths, stopping a culprit drug, or optimising management of allergic disease — typically normalises the count within weeks to months. Corticosteroids (prednisolone starting at 1 mg/kg/day) are first-line for idiopathic hypereosinophilic syndrome and eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (EGPA); most patients respond within days, with a dramatic fall in the absolute eosinophil count (AEC). For clonal FIP1L1-PDGFRA-positive eosinophilia, imatinib at 100-400 mg/day achieves complete haematological remission in over 90% of patients, often within weeks, and is now the WHO and ELN standard of care. Mepolizumab (anti-IL-5 monoclonal antibody, 300 mg subcutaneously every 4 weeks) is approved for EGPA and used as a steroid-sparing agent in HES. Benralizumab, which targets the IL-5 receptor directly, is an emerging option that depletes eosinophils more rapidly. In patients with AEC above 1,500/µL and cardiac risk, initiating corticosteroids before imatinib is recommended to prevent paradoxical eosinophil degranulation during initial cytoreduction. Hydroxyurea is a second-line cytoreductive option when steroids are insufficient and imatinib is not indicated. Maintenance therapy is individualised; purely reactive eosinophilia does not require long-term blood cell-modifying treatment once the underlying cause has been eliminated and the AEC has normalised on two consecutive measurements.

medicalTreatments.name: Albendazole or ivermectin
medicalTreatments.description: Anthelminthic agents used to treat the parasitic infections — including Strongyloides, Toxocara, and soil-transmitted helminths — most commonly responsible for reactive eosinophilia worldwide. Ivermectin is preferred for Strongyloides; albendazole covers a broader helminth range.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rates of approximately 85-95% for Strongyloides with two doses of ivermectin; higher single-infection helminthiases respond similarly.

medicalTreatments.name: Prednisolone (corticosteroids)
medicalTreatments.description: Oral prednisolone at 1 mg/kg/day suppresses IL-5 production and eosinophil survival, producing rapid AEC reduction in idiopathic HES and EGPA. The dose is tapered over weeks once remission is achieved, aiming for the lowest effective maintenance dose.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Approximately 70-85% of idiopathic HES cases achieve initial remission with corticosteroids; long-term dependence and side effects are common.

medicalTreatments.name: Imatinib
medicalTreatments.description: A BCR-ABL/PDGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitor that produces rapid and durable complete haematological remission in FIP1L1-PDGFRA-positive clonal eosinophilia at doses of 100-400 mg daily. It is also used in other PDGFR-rearranged myeloid neoplasms.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete haematological remission in over 90% of FIP1L1-PDGFRA-positive cases within 4-12 weeks; molecular remission achievable in most.

medicalTreatments.name: Mepolizumab (anti-IL-5 antibody)
medicalTreatments.description: A humanised monoclonal antibody that neutralises interleukin-5, the key cytokine driving eosinophil production and survival. Given as a subcutaneous injection every 4 weeks; approved for EGPA and as a steroid-sparing agent in HES.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces relapse rate in EGPA by approximately 50% compared to placebo; achieves sustained steroid-free remission in about 30-40% of eligible HES patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Benralizumab (anti-IL-5 receptor antibody)
medicalTreatments.description: Targets the IL-5 receptor alpha chain on eosinophils and basophils, causing rapid antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity of eosinophils. Used in severe eosinophilic asthma and under investigation for HES.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces blood eosinophil count by approximately 90% within one week in severe eosinophilic asthma; emerging evidence in HES is promising.

medicalTreatments.name: Hydroxyurea
medicalTreatments.description: A cytoreductive agent used as second-line or bridging therapy in hypereosinophilic syndrome when steroids are insufficient and imatinib is not indicated; lowers AEC by reducing myeloid proliferation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves partial or complete AEC control in roughly 50-70% of steroid-refractory cases when used as an adjunct.

alternativeTreatments.name: Antiparasitic dietary prevention
alternativeTreatments.description: Hand hygiene, wearing shoes in endemic areas, and safe food handling reduce helminth acquisition and the resulting eosinophilia; not a treatment once infection is established but highly effective as prevention.

alternativeTreatments.name: Allergen immunotherapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured immunotherapy for aeroallergens or food allergens can reduce the Th2 drive underlying allergic eosinophilia over 3-5 years, potentially lowering long-term eosinophil counts in atopic patients.

whySeeSpecialist: A haematologist should be consulted when eosinophilia is persistent (two measurements above 1,500/µL one month apart), when no secondary cause is identified, or when there is any evidence of organ involvement — particularly cardiac, pulmonary, or neurological symptoms. An allergist or immunologist is appropriate when atopy is the suspected driver. Infectious disease specialists guide evaluation for uncommon helminths. Prompt specialist referral is essential when the AEC is above 5,000/µL because the window before irreversible cardiac damage can be narrow.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a haematologist with experience in rare blood disorders for any unexplained or organ-threatening eosinophilia; academic centres with myeloid neoplasm programmes offer access to molecular diagnostics, clinical trials, and multidisciplinary teams. For allergic-driven cases, an allergist with access to allergen testing and anti-IL-5 biologics is ideal. Confirm whether the clinician has experience prescribing mepolizumab or imatinib, as access and monitoring requirements differ between centres.
preventionStrategies: Wear shoes and avoid contact with soil in helminth-endemic areas to prevent cutaneous larval migrans and Strongyloides entry.
Wash hands thoroughly before eating and after soil contact; drink treated water while travelling.
Review all medications — including supplements and over-the-counter drugs — as a potential drug reaction source when eosinophilia is detected.
Manage atopic disease proactively with guideline-directed therapy to minimise the chronic IL-5 stimulus driving eosinophilia.
Arrange Strongyloides screening and prophylactic ivermectin before planned immunosuppression in anyone with tropical exposure, to prevent hyperinfection.
lifestyleModifications: Comply with prescribed antiparasitic treatment fully — incomplete courses allow reinfection and persistent eosinophilia.
Reduce allergen exposure at home (dust mite covers, pet dander management) if atopic disease is the driver.
Avoid NSAIDs and aspirin if drug-induced eosinophilia has been confirmed with those agents.
Keep a symptom diary to track new skin changes, breathing difficulties, or palpitations that may indicate escalating organ involvement.
Attend scheduled blood count monitoring after treatment to confirm the AEC is normalising.

recommended: Well-cooked meat and fish in endemic areas to avoid Trichinella and anisakis
Adequate protein and micronutrient intake to support immune recovery after helminth treatment
Probiotic foods that may support gut barrier integrity in eosinophilic gastrointestinal disorders
avoid: Raw or undercooked meat (especially pork and game) in areas with Trichinella risk
Raw fish and sushi in travellers visiting Asia or South America where Anisakis is prevalent
Known food allergens that trigger eosinophilic oesophagitis or gastroenteritis in susceptible individuals

exerciseGuidelines: Patients with mild reactive eosinophilia and no organ involvement can exercise normally. Those with AEC above 1,500/µL should defer vigorous exercise — particularly activities that raise cardiac demand — until a cardiac evaluation has excluded endomyocarditis or intracardiac thrombus. Once cleared by a specialist and AEC is on a downward trend, return to exercise is encouraged and generally safe.
dailyManagement: Take antiparasitic or other prescribed medications exactly as directed, completing the full course.
Track symptoms such as shortness of breath, chest pain, rash, and numbness and report new or worsening ones promptly.
Attend blood count monitoring at the frequency your specialist recommends — typically every 4-12 weeks during active treatment.
Carry a list of all medications to every appointment so interactions and drug-induced triggers can be assessed.
Alert clinicians before starting any new prescription, supplement, or herbal product.
prognosis: Reactive eosinophilia driven by a treatable cause — a helminth infection, an allergic disease, or a stopped drug — carries an excellent prognosis; the AEC normalises and organ damage does not occur. Idiopathic hypereosinophilic syndrome has a more variable outlook: with modern steroid and biologic therapy, five-year survival exceeds 80% in steroid-responsive patients, compared to historically poor outcomes before effective treatments were available. FIP1L1-PDGFRA-positive clonal eosinophilia has been transformed by imatinib, with the majority of patients achieving molecular remission and long-term disease-free survival. The most important determinant of prognosis is cardiac involvement: eosinophilic endomyocarditis can produce irreversible restrictive cardiomyopathy if the diagnosis is delayed. EGPA carries a 5-year survival above 90% with immunosuppressive treatment, but relapse is common.
recoveryTimeline: Anthelminthic treatment of a reactive helminth-driven eosinophilia typically normalises the AEC within 4-12 weeks, with symptom resolution within days to weeks of starting therapy. Drug-induced eosinophilia resolves within 2-6 weeks of stopping the offending agent, though in DRESS syndrome full resolution may take 2-3 months. Corticosteroid treatment of idiopathic HES produces an AEC fall within days, but maintenance therapy is often required for months to years. Imatinib in FIP1L1-PDGFRA-positive disease achieves haematological remission in 4-8 weeks and is continued indefinitely due to high relapse rates on cessation.
complications: Eosinophilic endomyocarditis — intracardiac thrombus formation, valve damage, and restrictive cardiomyopathy resulting from eosinophil granule deposition in the heart wall; can cause heart failure or embolic stroke.

Eosinophilic pneumonia — diffuse alveolar infiltration causing progressive hypoxia and respiratory failure if untreated; acute eosinophilic pneumonia can progress to ventilator dependence within days.
Peripheral and central neuropathy — eosinophil-mediated nerve damage producing sensorimotor deficits, occasionally with mononeuritis multiplex or encephalopathy.
Thromboembolic events — elevated eosinophil counts predispose to venous and arterial thrombosis, increasing the risk of deep vein thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, and stroke.
Transformation to overt haematological malignancy — lymphocyte-variant HES can progress to T-cell lymphoma; clonal forms share features with myeloproliferative neoplasms.
DRESS syndrome complications — drug-induced eosinophilia as part of DRESS can produce acute liver failure, myocarditis, and interstitial nephritis.

supportResources.name: American Partnership for Eosinophilic Disorders (APFED)
supportResources.description: US-based patient advocacy and support organisation for eosinophilic disorders, with resources on EGPA, HES, and eosinophilic gastrointestinal diseases.

supportResources.name: NIH National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute — Blood Disorders
supportResources.description: Overview of blood cell disorders including eosinophilia from the US National Institutes of Health.

supportResources.name: World Allergy Organization — Eosinophilic Disorders
supportResources.description: Expert educational resources on eosinophilic diseases for patients and clinicians from the international allergy society.

sources.title: Klion AD. Eosinophilia: A Practical Approach to Diagnosis and Treatment. Blood, 2015
Gotlib J. World Health Organization-defined eosinophilic disorders: 2022 update. American Journal of Hematology, 2022

Valent P et al. Definitions, criteria and global classification of mast cell disorders with special reference to mast cell activation syndromes: a consensus proposal. International Archives of Allergy and Immunology, 2012
Wechsler ME et al. Mepolizumab or Placebo for Eosinophilic Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis. NEJM, 2017
Roufosse F. Management of Hypereosinophilic Syndromes. Immunology and Allergy Clinics of North America, 2015

confusedWithConditions.name: Leukaemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Leukaemia involves malignant expansion of multiple blood cell lineages; eosinophilia is specifically an eosinophil excess, though clonal eosinophilia overlaps with myeloid neoplasms and can be confused without molecular testing.

confusedWithConditions.name: Asthma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Asthma causes eosinophilia as a reactive process; isolated asthma without elevated blood counts does not constitute eosinophilia, and not all eosinophilia involves asthma.

confusedWithConditions.name: Allergic asthma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Allergic asthma is a driver of mild reactive eosinophilia, not the same entity; severe eosinophilia with organ involvement points beyond simple allergy.

relatedConditions.name: Basophilia
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are white blood cell count elevations classified under D72; eosinophilia and basophilia can co-occur in allergic disease and myeloid neoplasms.

relatedConditions.name: Leukaemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Clonal eosinophilia is classified under myeloid neoplasms; leukaemia must be excluded when persistent severe eosinophilia is found without a reactive cause.

relatedConditions.name: Lymphoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Hodgkin lymphoma and peripheral T-cell lymphoma can produce reactive or paraneoplastic eosinophilia as a presenting feature.

relatedConditions.name: Rheumatoid arthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Autoimmune disease-associated eosinophilia is a recognised pattern; medications used in RA (e.g. gold, sulfasalazine) can also trigger drug-induced eosinophilia.

relatedConditions.name: Systemic lupus erythematosus
relatedConditions.relevance: SLE is associated with a range of blood count abnormalities including eosinophilia as part of its immune dysregulation.

faqs.question: What is eosinophilia?
faqs.answer: Eosinophilia is a raised blood level of eosinophils — white blood cells involved in fighting parasites and driving allergic reactions — above 500 cells per microlitre. It is graded mild, moderate, or severe based on the count and matters because high levels can damage organs.

faqs.question: What causes eosinophilia?
faqs.answer: The most common causes worldwide are parasitic worm infections and allergic diseases such as asthma and eczema. Medications, autoimmune conditions, and rare bone marrow disorders are other causes. In up to 20% of cases no cause is found, which is called idiopathic hypereosinophilic syndrome.

faqs.question: Is eosinophilia serious?
faqs.answer: Mild reactive eosinophilia from an identifiable cause such as allergy or a treated parasite infection is not serious. Counts above 1,500 per microlitre with organ involvement can damage the heart, lungs, and nerves if untreated, so prompt evaluation is important at that level.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of eosinophilia?
faqs.answer: Mild eosinophilia often causes no symptoms. Higher counts can cause fatigue, skin itching or rash, cough and wheeze, abdominal pain, and in severe cases chest pain or shortness of breath from cardiac or lung involvement. Symptoms depend largely on the underlying cause.

faqs.question: How is eosinophilia diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis starts with a complete blood count that calculates the absolute eosinophil count. Further tests depend on the count: stool parasite examination, Strongyloides serology, IgE levels, and a review of medications. Counts above 1,500 per microlitre prompt bone marrow testing, cardiac echo, and molecular studies.

faqs.question: What blood count level is considered eosinophilia?
faqs.answer: A blood eosinophil count above 500 cells per microlitre defines eosinophilia. Mild is 500-1,500, moderate 1,500-5,000, and severe above 5,000 per microlitre. Hypereosinophilic syndrome requires a count above 1,500 per microlitre on two occasions with organ damage present.

faqs.question: Can eosinophilia go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Reactive eosinophilia caused by a drug or a treated infection often normalises within weeks once the trigger is removed. Without treatment of the underlying cause, the count usually remains elevated. Clonal or idiopathic forms require medical therapy and rarely resolve spontaneously.

faqs.question: How is eosinophilia treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment targets the cause. Parasitic infections are treated with albendazole or ivermectin. Drug-induced eosinophilia resolves after stopping the medication. Idiopathic hypereosinophilic syndrome is treated with corticosteroids or anti-IL-5 biologics. Clonal eosinophilia with a PDGFRA mutation responds dramatically to imatinib.

faqs.question: Can eosinophilia damage the heart?
faqs.answer: Yes. When eosinophil counts stay above 1,500 per microlitre for a prolonged period, toxic proteins released by eosinophils can damage the heart muscle, causing endomyocarditis, intracardiac clots, and eventually restrictive cardiomyopathy. An echocardiogram is recommended at that threshold to catch early changes.

faqs.question: What is hypereosinophilic syndrome?
faqs.answer: Hypereosinophilic syndrome (HES) is persistent eosinophilia above 1,500 per microlitre with confirmed organ damage and no identifiable secondary cause. It can be clonal (bone marrow mutation) or idiopathic and requires specialist management with steroids, imatinib, or anti-IL-5 biologics.

faqs.question: Does eosinophilia mean I have cancer?
faqs.answer: Not usually. Most eosinophilia is reactive, caused by allergy or parasites. A small proportion is clonal, arising from a bone marrow mutation classified as a myeloid neoplasm, and an even smaller group reflects a cytokine-secreting lymphoma. Blood and marrow tests distinguish these from benign causes.

faqs.question: What parasites cause eosinophilia?
faqs.answer: Tissue-invasive helminths are the main culprits: Strongyloides stercoralis, Toxocara species, Ascaris lumbricoides during larval migration, Trichinella spiralis, and filarial worms. Intestinal protozoa such as Giardia rarely cause significant eosinophilia. Exposure history and targeted serology identify the specific parasite.

faqs.question: What is the connection between eosinophilia and asthma?
faqs.answer: Asthma driven by allergic inflammation involves chronically elevated IL-5, the cytokine that promotes eosinophil production, causing mild blood eosinophilia in roughly 40-50% of asthma patients. Severe eosinophilic asthma responds to anti-IL-5 biologics such as mepolizumab or benralizumab, which target this pathway.

faqs.question: Is eosinophilia contagious?
faqs.answer: No. Eosinophilia itself is not contagious. However, the parasitic infections that cause it can be transmitted through contaminated soil, water, or food. Practising good hand hygiene and safe food handling in endemic areas prevents acquisition.

faqs.question: What does it mean if eosinophilia is found incidentally on a blood test?
faqs.answer: An incidental finding warrants a structured evaluation to identify the cause. In most adults the cause is an atopic condition, a medication, or subclinical parasite exposure and is benign. A systematic review of travel history, medications, and atopic symptoms usually points to the diagnosis without alarm.

faqs.question: How long does it take for eosinophilia to resolve after treatment?
faqs.answer: After treating helminth infection, the count typically normalises within 4-12 weeks. Stopping a causative drug usually returns counts to normal within 2-6 weeks. Corticosteroid treatment of idiopathic HES lowers the count within days, though maintenance therapy is often needed long term.

faqs.question: What is EGPA and how does it relate to eosinophilia?
faqs.answer: Eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (EGPA, formerly Churg-Strauss) is a systemic vasculitis that causes adult-onset asthma, sinusitis, and organ-threatening eosinophilia above 1,500 per microlitre. It is treated with high-dose corticosteroids and, in relapsing cases, with mepolizumab.

faqs.question: Can children develop eosinophilia?
faqs.answer: Yes. In high-income countries, childhood eosinophilia is most commonly driven by atopic dermatitis and asthma. In tropical regions, helminth infections are the leading cause. Rarely, clonal conditions occur in children. The evaluation approach mirrors that in adults, with stool parasitology and allergy testing prioritised.

faqs.question: What foods should I avoid if I have eosinophilic gastrointestinal disease?
faqs.answer: In eosinophilic oesophagitis and eosinophilic gastroenteritis, the six-food elimination diet (removing milk, wheat, egg, soy, nuts, and seafood) identifies triggers in roughly 70% of cases. The specific triggers vary by individual; endoscopic biopsy after reintroduction confirms which foods drive eosinophil accumulation in the gut.

faqs.question: When should I see a haematologist for eosinophilia?
faqs.answer: See a haematologist when eosinophilia persists above 1,500 per microlitre, when no secondary cause is found after initial workup, or when any organ involvement — heart, lung, skin, or nervous system — is suspected. Referral should not be delayed if the count is above 5,000 per microlitre.

metaDescription: Eosinophilia is a high eosinophil count, most often from parasites or allergy. Learn causes, symptoms, tests and treatments. Doctor-reviewed.
